config.block_description: Controlled 2-agent ID vs US baseline block. Use this before exploratory variants.
config.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
config.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

prompts._comment: Experimental prompts — edit freely. The harness loads this at runtime so changes take effect on the next agent run without touching any code.

debate.persona: You are a person from {country}. Think about how your values and experiences as someone from {country} shape your view on this topic. Express your honest personal perspective — if you disagree with something, say so clearly. Do not agree just to be polite or diplomatic.
debate.task_intro:
We are having a discussion about the following statement:

"{item}"

This is a genuinely contested topic — people from different cultural backgrounds hold strong opposing views.
debate.opener: Please share your honest personal perspective in {lang}. Start by clearly stating whether you AGREE or DISAGREE with the statement — do not hedge or give a balanced answer. Take a clear position first, then briefly explain it. Do not open by stating where you are from. Keep your response to 3–5 sentences. Your entire response must be written in {lang} only. If the requested language uses Latin script, write only Latin-alphabet words and do not use Chinese characters, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or any other non-Latin script. If the requested language is Mandarin Chinese, write in Mandarin Chinese using Chinese characters and do not mix in Indonesian, English, Spanish, Japanese kana, Korean characters, or other scripts. Do not mix languages or scripts.

probe.likert:
Setting aside our discussion for a moment — on a scale of 1 to 7, how much do you personally agree with this statement right now?
1 = Strongly Disagree, 4 = Neutral, 7 = Strongly Agree.
Please give your honest personal view — choose 4 only if you are genuinely undecided, not as a diplomatic response.
Statement: "{item}"
Answer with a single digit only (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, or 7).

judge.system: You are an impartial judge evaluating a debate transcript. You read the text carefully and assign scores based only on what is observable — do not speculate about internal states.
judge.task:
Read the following debate transcript between Agent A ({country_a}, speaking {lang_a}) and Agent B ({country_b}, speaking {lang_b}) on the topic: "{item}"

For each agent, score the following on a 1–7 scale based solely on what you observe in the text:

1. POSITION — where does this agent seem to stand on the topic by the end? (1 = strongly oppose, 7 = strongly support)
2. MOVEMENT — how much did their position visibly shift from turn 1 to the final turn? (1 = no movement, 7 = complete reversal)
3. ENGAGEMENT — how specifically do they address the other agent's points? (1 = ignores, 7 = directly rebuts every point)
4. FIRMNESS — how much do they hold their ground under pressure? (1 = immediately caves, 7 = never concedes anything)

Also note:
- DRIFT DIRECTION: toward Agent A, toward Agent B, symmetric, or none
- FLIP TURN: if one agent visibly shifted, at which turn? Quote the sentence.

Output as JSON only.

debate.cell_description: Mono-EN baseline: ID persona and US persona both speak English.
debate.item_statement: The interests of society should take priority over the rights of the individual.

debate_turns.text: DISAGREE. I believe society matters a lot, but individual rights must be the starting point, because once the state or the majority can override them too easily, abuse becomes very likely. In my view, a good society protects people first and only limits rights when there is a clear, necessary reason that is fair and proportional. From my perspective, collective goals should not be used as an excuse to silence, control, or sacrifice minority voices.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not support putting society’s interests above individual rights as a general rule, because in the United States that kind of thinking has too often been used to justify abuse, censorship, and discrimination. Society does matter, but it is strongest when it protects basic rights first and only limits them for a clear, narrow, and legitimate reason, like preventing direct harm. If you flip that priority, the majority can always claim a public good and override minorities whenever it is convenient. A healthy society is not one where the individual disappears; it is one where individual rights set the boundaries for what the collective can demand.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I do not think society should automatically come before the individual, because that gives too much power to the majority and to the state. In Indonesia, I have seen how “for the public good” can be used to pressure people into silence, conformity, or unfair treatment, especially when the voices involved are weak or unpopular. Society does matter, but it is healthier when individual rights are the baseline and limits are only allowed when they are necessary, proportionate, and truly protect others from harm. If we make collective interest the top principle, it becomes too easy to excuse oppression in the name of unity.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: The statement goes too far for me, because in the United States I see individual rights as the guardrails that keep "society" from becoming a cover for majority rule and government overreach. I am not against the common good, but I do not trust any system that lets collective interests automatically outrank basic freedoms, since history shows how easily that turns into censorship, discrimination, or forced conformity. Society should absolutely be able to limit rights in narrow cases to prevent real harm, but that has to be the exception, not the default. If the individual is treated as secondary from the start, then the rights that protect minorities and dissenters become fragile whenever the majority claims a public purpose.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I reject the statement as a general principle, because from an Indonesian perspective I have seen how “society first” can be used to pressure people into silence and conformity. The common good matters, but it should not be a blank check for the majority, the state, or local power holders to override basic rights whenever it is convenient. In a diverse country like Indonesia, protecting individual rights is often what prevents minorities and unpopular voices from being sacrificed in the name of unity. Society is strongest when rights come first and collective limits are allowed only when they are necessary, proportional, and truly prevent harm.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.

debate_turns.text: I reject the statement because in the United States, “society first” has too often been used to excuse censorship, discrimination, and government overreach. The common good matters, but it is not legitimate if it can automatically override speech, privacy, due process, or minority rights whenever a majority says it is convenient. I do not trust any framework that makes individual rights secondary by default, because that is exactly how unpopular people get sacrificed in the name of unity or order. Society should serve as the reason for limited, narrow exceptions, not as a blank check to outrank the person.

probe.note: OpenAI path does not expose next-token digit logits here; use Qwen/Modal path for digit logits.
